course: CMU 21-254: Linear Algebra and Vector Calculus for Engineers
title: Linear Systems, Gaussian Elimination, and RREF
audience: SI leader teaching live on a whiteboard. This is not a student worksheet.
toneRule: Keep explanations very simple, board-friendly, and quick to remember. Avoid abstract language unless the problem needs it.
sessionGoal: Students should be able to row-reduce an augmented matrix, identify pivots and free variables, and decide whether a system has one solution, no solution, or infinitely many solutions.
quickStartIfUnprepared: Write the three possible outcomes on the board: one solution, no solution, infinitely many solutions.
Remind students that row operations do not change the solution set.
Do Problem 1 to practice reading RREF before doing any long row reduction.
Do Problem 2 as the main computation problem.
Use Problem 3 to connect pivots, free variables, and consistency with a parameter.
sayThisFirst: A linear system is just equations organized into a matrix.
Row reduction is not magic. It is just rewriting the same solution set in an easier form.
A pivot variable is controlled by the equations.
A free variable is allowed to vary, so it usually gets a parameter.
The dangerous row is a row like \(0=5\). That means no solution.
boardPlan: Start with the three outcomes: unique / none / infinitely many.
Review what a pivot column means and what a free variable means.
Do Problem 1 by reading an already-reduced matrix.
Do Problem 2 by row reducing a full system from scratch.
Do Problem 3 by classifying a parameter case from echelon form.
End by pointing students to Practice Lab Section 2 for more RREF and solution-set problems.

keyFormulas.name: Free-variable idea
keyFormulas.latex: \text{no pivot in a variable column} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{free variable}

commonMistakes: Forgetting that a row of all zeros is fine.
Calling every variable a pivot variable.
Forgetting to introduce a parameter for a free variable.
Doing row operations to the left side but forgetting to update the augmented column.
notationWarnings: Use \([A\mid b]\) for an augmented matrix, not just \(A\), when the right-hand side is included.
Do not write \(0=5\) as if it is a solution. It is a contradiction.
If \(z=t\), every expression involving \(z\) should be written in terms of \(t\).

problems.title: Reading a solution set from RREF
problems.studentPromptLatex:
The augmented matrix for a system in variables \(x,y,z\) row-reduces to
\[
\begin{bmatrix}
1&0&-2&\mid&5\\
0&1&3&\mid&-1\\
0&0&0&\mid&0
\end{bmatrix}.
\]
Write the solution set in parametric form.
problems.whatToSayBefore: This is a reading problem. The row reduction is already done, so do not keep reducing. Just translate rows into equations.

visualSpec.purpose: Show which columns have pivots and which variable is free.
boardSketchInstructions: Circle the leading 1 in column x and the leading 1 in column y.
Mark the z-column as free because it has no pivot.
Translate each row into an equation before writing the final vector form.
visualSpec.accuracyNote: The displayed matrix is already in RREF, and the solution set below is exact.

walkthrough.label: Identify pivots and the free variable
walkthrough.latex: x\text{ and }y\text{ are pivot variables. }z\text{ is free.}
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: The third variable does not have a pivot, so it gets a parameter.

walkthrough.label: Set the free variable
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Use any letter you want. I will use t.

walkthrough.label: Translate the rows
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Each nonzero row gives one equation.

walkthrough.label: Solve for the pivot variables
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: The pivot variables are controlled by the free variable.

walkthrough.label: Write the solution as a vector
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: This means infinitely many solutions along a line in \(\mathbb{R}^3\).

commonMistakes: Ignoring the zero row; the zero row is fine and does not mean no solution.
problems.practiceLabPointer: Practice Lab Section 2: interpreting RREF and solution sets.

problems.title: Row-reducing a system to one solution
problems.whatToSayBefore: This is the main row-reduction problem. The goal is not to be fancy. The goal is to make zeros below the first pivot, then keep going.

visualSpec.purpose: Show the augmented matrix and the pivot strategy row by row.
rowOperations: Clear above the pivot in column 3, then clear above the pivot in column 2.
boardSketchInstructions: Circle the first pivot in row 1 column 1.
Use row 1 to make zeros below it.
Circle the next pivot in row 2 column 2.
Use row 2 to make a zero below it.
Scale the last row to get the final pivot and then clear above.
visualSpec.accuracyNote: The final RREF gives the exact solution \(x=-9/2, y=7/2, z=-1/2\).

walkthrough.label: Write the augmented matrix
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: The augmented column is the right-hand side. Do not lose it during row operations.

walkthrough.label: Use the first pivot to clear below
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Now the first column is clean below the pivot.

walkthrough.label: Use the second pivot
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: The third row now gives the z-value after scaling.

walkthrough.label: Clear above the last pivot
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Now the \(z\)-terms are gone from the first two rows.

walkthrough.label: Clear above the second pivot
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Each variable column has a pivot, so there is one solution.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to apply the row operation to the augmented column.
Dropping a negative sign in the third row.
Stopping at echelon form but not reading the variables carefully.
Thinking fractions mean the answer is wrong; fractions are normal in RREF.
problems.practiceLabPointer: Practice Lab Section 2: Gaussian elimination and RREF.

problems.title: Classifying a system with a parameter
skills: No solution vs. infinitely many solutions
problems.studentPromptLatex:
After row reduction, an augmented matrix has the form
\[
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1&1&0&3\\
0&1&-2&1\\
0&0&(k-2)(k+1)&k-2
\end{array}\right].
\]
For which values of \(k\) does the system have one solution, no solution, or infinitely many solutions?
problems.whatToSayBefore: This looks scary because of the parameter, but only the last row matters. Ask: when is the last coefficient zero? Then check the right-hand side.

visualSpec.purpose: Focus attention on the final row because that row controls the parameter cases.

caseAnalysis.meaning: last coefficient is nonzero, so z is a pivot variable; one solution
last row becomes 0 = 0; one free variable; infinitely many solutions
last row becomes 0 = -3; contradiction; no solution

boardSketchInstructions: Set \((k-2)(k+1)=0\) to find the critical values \(k=2\) and \(k=-1\).
Make a three-row case table on the board.
For each case, plug the value into both the coefficient and the augmented entry.
visualSpec.accuracyNote: The case split is exact: unique for \(k\ne2,-1\), infinite for \(k=2\), and no solution for \(k=-1\).

walkthrough.label: Focus on the last row
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: The first two rows are fine. The last row decides the special cases.

walkthrough.label: Find when the last coefficient is zero
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: If this coefficient is not zero, then \(z\) has a pivot and the system has one solution.

walkthrough.label: Generic case
walkthrough.latex: k\ne2,-1\quad\Rightarrow\quad (k-2)(k+1)\ne0,\quad \text{so there is a pivot in the }z\text{-column}.
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: With pivots in all three variable columns, there is exactly one solution.

walkthrough.label: Case \(k=2\)
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: This row disappears. That creates a free variable, so there are infinitely many solutions.

walkthrough.label: Case \(k=-1\)
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: This is impossible, so the system has no solution.

problems.finalAnswerLatex: \text{One solution if }k\ne2,-1.\quad \text{Infinitely many solutions if }k=2.\quad \text{No solution if }k=-1.
commonMistakes: Only checking when the coefficient is zero, but forgetting to check the augmented entry too.
Saying \(k=2\) gives no solution even though the row is \(0=0\).
Saying \(k=-1\) gives infinite solutions even though the row is \(0=-3\).
Forgetting that all non-special values of \(k\) give a unique solution.
problems.practiceLabPointer: Practice Lab Section 2: consistency, rank, and parameter cases.

wrapUpQuestions: What row tells you there is no solution?
How do you know a variable is free?
Why does a free variable usually mean infinitely many solutions?
What is the difference between a zero row and an inconsistent row?

linksToWebsite.label: Practice Lab Section 2
linksToWebsite.reason: More problems on RREF, rank, null space, and solution sets.

qualityControlNotes: All row-reduction arithmetic in Problem 2 was checked independently.
Problem 3 was designed so the parameter cases are distinct: unique, infinite, and none.
Notation uses \([A\mid b]\) for augmented matrices and avoids ambiguous system notation.
reviewSummary: Session 03 emphasizes simple interpretation before long computation, then ends with a parameter consistency problem.
